theory_name: Hybrid Structured-Episodic Memory as a Fundamental Requirement for Robust Performance in LLM-Based Text Game Agents
theory_description: This theory posits that robust performance, generalization, and strategic flexibility in language model (LLM) agents across complex, partially observable text games require not just large memory but a principled integration of structured (e.g., knowledge/belief graph) and episodic/reflection-based memory. The synergy emerges because structured memory offers compact relational representations for navigation and reasoning, while episodic/reflection memory provides dynamic adaptation, procedural recall, and learning from both failure and success. Neither naive retention of all history nor pure reliance on unstructured episodic memory alone is sufficient for scaling or generalizing to new, difficult, or long-horizon tasks; selective retrieval and composition of these modalities is necessary to balance information compression, retrieval relevance, and depth of reasoning.

law.statement_name: Hybrid Structured-Episodic Memory Yields Superior Generalization and Sample Efficiency
law.theory_statement: LLM-based agents in high-dimensional, partially observable, multi-step text games achieve substantially better generalization, faster convergence, and higher ultimate performance when equipped with hybrid memory architectures that combine structured semantic state representations (e.g., knowledge/belief graphs) with episodic/reflection-based memory modules for experience and procedural recall, as compared to agents relying on a single memory modality or naive in-context windowing.
law.domain_scope: Applies to LLM-based agents in interactive fiction (IF), text-adventure, multi-agent simulations, and real or synthetic multi-step text games featuring partial observability, complex environments, sparse/deferred rewards, compositional tasks (e.g., navigation, inventory management), and long planning horizons.
special_cases: In environments that are fully observable with trivial state/action structure, single-modality or no memory augmentation suffices.
Tasks requiring only short-term recall (≤ context window) see little or no benefit from hybridization.

supporting_evidence.text: Ariadne/AriGraph integrates knowledge graph and episodic memory; outperforming all baselines including those with full-history or reflection/episodic memory only. Ablations (disabling episodic or using full-history, RAG, iterative summarization, or Simulacra) always underperform.

GATA, KG-A2C, MoE Thespian and extension agents with knowledge graph consistently outperform non-structured baselines and text-only RL or episodic-only agents in benchmarks such as TextWorld, Jericho, LIGHT, and custom role-playing worlds.

Generative Agents (Smallville) and MemInsight separately demonstrate the necessity to combine different types of memory (e.g., long-term episodic memory with reflections/plans or attribute-augmented retrieval) for persisting social identity and reasoning fidelity.
MC-DML and RAP show that integrating both cross-trial/failure reflections and short-term trajectory memory yields the highest gains over baselines with only single-type memory.

Reflection-only (Reflexion), full-history, unstructured episodic, count-based, or summarization-only memory fail to match hybrid/structured performance and scalability, especially on long-horizon, multi-step, or compositional tasks (e.g., Cleaning, NetHack, Cooking Hard, Treasure Hunt Hard).

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Hybrid memory integration has been implemented in some prior systems (e.g., AriGraph), discussed in cognitive science, and episodic/semantic separation in RL and memory-augmented models, but usually as design rationale or agent details—rarely as a formal requirement or general law.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: This law synthesizes and formalizes, at the algorithmic level, that hybridization (not mere addition) of structured/graph and episodic/reflection memory is a necessary condition for robust scalable performance in LLM text-game agents, based on exhaustive comparative evidence.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Existing works support the principle in context, but this is the first systematic, general law stated as both necessary and sufficient for task class and performance regime.
references: Papazian et al. (2024) AriGraph [hybrid agent shows SOTA but not generalized as law]
Park et al. (2023) Generative Agents [episodic+reflection memory for social simulation, unstructured]
Meng et al. (2025) MemInsight [structured augmentation for retrieval]
Wang et al. (2020) Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs [structured belief graph, not hybrid law]

theory_name: Hybrid Structured-Episodic Memory as a Fundamental Requirement for Robust Performance in LLM-Based Text Game Agents
theory_description: This theory posits that robust performance, generalization, and strategic flexibility in language model (LLM) agents across complex, partially observable text games require not just large memory but a principled integration of structured (e.g., knowledge/belief graph) and episodic/reflection-based memory. The synergy emerges because structured memory offers compact relational representations for navigation and reasoning, while episodic/reflection memory provides dynamic adaptation, procedural recall, and learning from both failure and success. Neither naive retention of all history nor pure reliance on unstructured episodic memory alone is sufficient for scaling or generalizing to new, difficult, or long-horizon tasks; selective retrieval and composition of these modalities is necessary to balance information compression, retrieval relevance, and depth of reasoning.

law.statement_name: Memory Structure, Selectivity, and Retrieval are as Critical as Memory Size or Modality
law.theory_statement: The benefit of memory augmentation to LLM agents in text games depends not just on the presence or volume of stored experience, but crucially on how memory is structured (graphs, attributes, summaries), accessed (contextual semantic retrieval, attribute filtering, embedding similarity, graph traversal), and composed/interleaved with ongoing reasoning. Unstructured, brute-force context window or naive concatenation yields information overload or sparsity and fails to scale in complex games.
law.domain_scope: LLM-based agents with any form of persistent memory in settings with nontrivial state/action space, partial observability, and long or branching planning horizons.
special_cases: In trivial or small-scale settings where history fits in the prompt, advanced retrieval offers little marginal gain.
In environments with no discoverable structure or pure randomness, selectivity/structure add overhead but little task benefit.

supporting_evidence.text: Worldformer’s graph-diff representation and set-of-sequences (SOS) loss, RAP’s windowed trajectory retrieval, MemInsight’s attribute-based and embedding retrieval, and MC-DML’s distinction between context window and cross-trial memory all yield large performance improvements over naive retrieval or memory-dumping agents.
Ariadne/AriGraph's ablations (full-history, summaries, RAG, Simulacra) and unstructured context concatenation produce much lower performance and poor scaling to complex or long tasks.
ThinkThrice and Sweet&Sour explicitly leverage retrieval, selective reflection, and verification to outperform memoryless or unselective/naive retrieval agents.

GATA, KG-A2C, and MoE agents' use of graph-based masking, attention, and value-seeded mixing demonstrates the criticality of retrieval mechanisms and selective structuring, as opposed to raw memory augmentation.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Importance of retrieval is acknowledged in information retrieval, RAG, and some memory-augmented LM architectures; information overload in full context is a recognized issue.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: This general law formalizes the equivalence (or superiority) of memory structure and access relative to raw memory volume or memory type for performance/goals in complex reasoning environments, based on unified evidence.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Evidence exists in various ablations and isolated system papers, but this theory codifies it at a high level and ties it to both failure and success modes systematically.
references: Borji et al. (2023) A Survey on Large Language Model-Based Game Agents [retrieval cited, not law]
Meng et al. (2025) MemInsight [attribute/embedding retrieval]
Yao et al. (2023) RAG survey and retrieval theory

theory_name: Information Compression and History Representation are Key Limiting Factors in LLM Agent Memory Utility
theory_description: This theory asserts that in text-based game environments with large, high-dimensional or verbose observations, the utility of memory for LLM agents is fundamentally constrained by the information-theoretic compressibility (i.e., sufficient representation) of experience. Explicitly, compressive forms of context/history (e.g., graph-diff, Unix diff, structured summaries) vastly outperform equally-sized or even much larger uncompressed or full-history approaches, by ensuring that relevant signals outlast window constraints and guide agent policy effectively. Token budget, compression ratio, and choice of memory summarization directly determine effective planning horizon and strategic depth.

law.statement_name: Memory Compression Ratio Determines Planning Depth and Performance
law.theory_statement: The effective planning and recall horizon of LLM-based agents in text games is quantitatively bounded by the compression ratio of the memory representation: Given a fixed token/context size, agents with higher compression modes (e.g., diff, graph-diff, structured area/summary) can represent deeper and more salient past experience in context, yielding higher sample efficiency, faster convergence, and better asymptotic performance than full-history or verbose episodic memory agents.
law.domain_scope: Applies to any LLM-based agent in settings with high-dimensional, verbose, or rapidly growing history (e.g., NetHack, complex IF, games with combinatorial state), especially when context/prompt size is a bottleneck.
special_cases: In environments with inherently low-dimensional state (e.g., small grids), the benefits of compression are diminished.
If relevant information is single-step and always present, compression gives marginal value.

supporting_evidence.text: Diff history (Unix diff deltas) in NetHack reduces average tokens per observation by 460%, allowing for commensurately longer context window and leading to a ~60-fold performance improvement versus full-text history; full-text history agents collapse after <10 steps, while diff-history succeeds with up to 15-step horizon.
Worldformer’s graph-diff representation provides large gains in both action and graph prediction, outperforming token-long, full-graph or standard seq2seq baselines.
Area-selection (fixed-size substate) and structured summarization in Goedendag enables bounded growth and low error: omitting area-selection (ablation) causes ~5x error increase.
Full-history baseline in AriGraph uses up to 14,000 tokens in 150 steps, yet performs poorly (scores <0.5 normalized), compared to structured/summary/context-limited agents.

BabyAI-Text: diff-history saves 25% compute to reach the same performance as full-history, despite lower dimension; combining compression with auxiliary prediction objectives sometimes further improves learning (Full-text+aux).

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Compression and summarization have a long history in NLP and model memory, but are rarely formalized as fundamental bottlenecks limiting effective context and depth for agent planning.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: This law quantitatively links chosen memory compression ratio (not just format) to sample efficiency, planning depth, and performance ceiling, and synthesizes ablation and scaling evidence into a single predictive law.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Compression’s utility is empirically known for text processing, but this context-window-and-depth law as a governing principle for agent design is new here.
references: Papazian et al. (2023) diff History [applies diff, but does not generalize as law]
Zhou et al. (2021) Worldformer [advocates graph-diff for efficiency, not strict necessity]
Park et al. (2023) Generative Agents [summarization/planning, but not formal compression law]

theory_name: Experience-Driven Memory Compression and Selective Retention as Core Drivers of Scalability and Efficiency in LLM Text Game Agents
theory_description: LLM-based agents in text games achieve scalable, efficient, and generalizable behavior not by brute-force expansion of memory or parameters, but through two foundational strategies: (1) compressing and abstracting episodic information—via methods such as diffs, structured summaries, or attribute-value augmentation—thereby maximizing the functional memory horizon given context or compute limits, and (2) selecting and retaining only the most valuable episodes (e.g., successful, instructive, or high-signal experiences), which focuses agent updates and retrieval on what is most relevant for improvement. These principles, when systematically implemented, enable agents to surpass the limitations of naive context concatenation or indiscriminate storage, yielding significant gains in sample efficiency, planning horizon, and generalization to novel tasks.

law.theory_statement: Employing information-theoretic compression or abstraction mechanisms—including, but not limited to, diff encoding, structured summarization, graph-diff prediction, and attribute-value annotation—directly increases the effective memory horizon and sample efficiency of LLM agents in high-dimensional, sequential text environments, compared with raw history concatenation.
law.domain_scope: LLM agents in environments where state/observation sequences are lengthy, high-dimensional, partially observable, or verbose (e.g., NetHack, multi-stage text games, dialogue-heavy simulations), and naive memory exceeds context or resource limitations.
special_cases: In environments with very low observation entropy or extremely short episodes, compression imparts minimal or negligible gains over naive memory.
If the compressed format omits critical task-relevant features (e.g., action affordances), excessive or naïve compression can degrade performance.

supporting_evidence.text: Diff history agents massively outperform raw full-text history agents on NetHack and achieve SOTA performance on low-data tasks; diff compresses token usage by up to 460% and enables extended memory windows.

MemInsight's structured attribute-value memory and attribute-based/embedding-based retrieval lead to much higher F1/Recall in QA and recommendation tasks over raw memory bank, RAG, or MemoryBank baselines.
Worldformer achieves state-of-the-art KG prediction by predicting graph diffs and avoiding redundant storage versus full-graph Seq2Seq or QA-based KG methods.
Summarization agents (Structured game rep(G)) and area selection (in ToT/Goedendag) effectively constrain and compress state for reasoning, vastly lowering error rates and memory growth.

Summary memory (iterative summarization) provides better context efficiency than full history in partial observability but can underperform structured graph-based memory and is sensitive to the summarization method.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Diffs, attribute-based memory, graph diffs, and various summarization methods have been deployed in isolated systems, but typically as optimizations rather than formalized memory principles.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: This statement abstracts and unifies compression and structured abstraction across diverse methods as a necessary and foundational tenet for memory scaling in text-game agents.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Compression strategies appear separately in recent LLM-for-games literature, but not as a general, unifying law explaining when/why scalability emerges.
references: Papazian et al. (2023) diff History for Neural Language Agents [diff-based compression]
Meng et al. (2025) MemInsight [attribute-value compression, efficient retrieval]
Wang et al. (2021) Learning Knowledge Graph-based World Models [graph diff and summarization]

theory_name: Experience-Driven Memory Compression and Selective Retention as Core Drivers of Scalability and Efficiency in LLM Text Game Agents
theory_description: LLM-based agents in text games achieve scalable, efficient, and generalizable behavior not by brute-force expansion of memory or parameters, but through two foundational strategies: (1) compressing and abstracting episodic information—via methods such as diffs, structured summaries, or attribute-value augmentation—thereby maximizing the functional memory horizon given context or compute limits, and (2) selecting and retaining only the most valuable episodes (e.g., successful, instructive, or high-signal experiences), which focuses agent updates and retrieval on what is most relevant for improvement. These principles, when systematically implemented, enable agents to surpass the limitations of naive context concatenation or indiscriminate storage, yielding significant gains in sample efficiency, planning horizon, and generalization to novel tasks.

law.statement_name: Selective Experience Retention Dominates Uniform or Unfiltered Memory Strategies
law.theory_statement: Preferentially storing and retrieving only those episodes with high learning value—such as successful task trajectories, positive/negative subgoal reflections, or important/prioritized attribute memories—confers significantly faster and more robust agent learning, generalization, and planning than retaining all (uninformatively) or using volume-based storage; in many cases, this can outweigh the effect of simply increasing model size or raw memory.
law.domain_scope: LLM-based agents (and RL+LM hybrids) in multi-step, sparse-reward, or strategy-intensive text environments, including settings with large experience imbalance or long exploration horizons.
special_cases: If most experiences are equally informative (e.g., random, single-step games), selection gives little to no advantage.
When informative signals cannot be determined or are not reflected in observed rewards or outcomes, selection is limited in benefit.

supporting_evidence.text: RAP agent retrieves only successful prior trajectory windows, yielding much higher success rates on ALFWorld and WebShop than agents using reflection-only, ReAct, or uniform retrieval.

Sweet&Sour achieves large performance gains over failure-only or unfiltered reflective memory methods by explicitly storing and sampling both positive and negative experiences; ablating positive reflections greatly reduces agent performance.
Iterated Action Modeling (successful-demo replay) boosts few-shot ALFWorld GPT2-agent performance, shrinking demo-scarcity gap.

Reflection-only methods (Reflexion) that fail to encode positive feedback or do not select for informative failures perform much worse, can tilt or get stuck in local minima, and underperform RAP and Sweet&Sour.
Attribute-based filtering (MemInsight) further increases retrieval effectiveness over bulk/sequence-oriented memory (high recall and F1).

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Experience replay and prioritized replay (by TD/error, etc.) have been standard in RL, but not widely formalized for reflection, positive/negative selection, or structured retrieval in LLM memory.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: This law recasts prioritization as a universal learning-efficiency principle across LLM, reflectional, and hybrid agent memory systems; generalizes beyond classic TD-based selection to qualitative, structural, or reward-agnostic selectors.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Adopts concepts from RL and memory-augmented LLMs, uniting them as a general principle, not just an implementation detail.
references: Shinn et al. (2023) Reflexion [Failure reflection, limited positive/structured selection]
Papazian et al. (2024) Sweet&Sour [Managed memory and positive/negative selection, large delta]
Papazian et al. (2024) RAP [Successful trajectory filtering for planning]

theory_name: Structured Semantic Memory (Knowledge Graphs and Attribute-augmented Episodic Stores) is Essential for Long-horizon Planning and Generalization in LLM Text Game Agents
theory_description: LLM agents provided with structured, semantic memory formats—such as knowledge graphs (explicitly or via belief graphs), hybrid semantic-episodic graphs, or attribute-annotated episodic memory—enable richer, multi-hop reasoning about state, navigation, and plans than either unstructured episodic or pure summary memory. The graph or attribute structure not only supports efficient navigation and state tracking in partially observable games, but also enables multi-step reasoning, ensures consistency across steps, and enhances the generalization of agent policies to novel environments and tasks.

law.statement_name: Structured Graph or Attribute-based Memories Outperform Unstructured or Plain Episodic Memory in Multi-step Reasoning and Generalization
law.theory_statement: Agents that employ explicit, structured semantic memory (such as knowledge graphs, hybrid semantic-episodic graphs, or attribute-augmented stores) achieve higher scores, faster convergence, and more robust generalization across unseen or complex tasks compared to agents using raw full-history, simple episodic note storage, or iterative summary memory.
law.domain_scope: Text-based sequential environments with partial observability, complex navigation, multi-step objectives, or sparse feedback—e.g., TextWorld, NetHack, ALFWorld, and multi-agent simulation domains.
special_cases: If task structure is trivial, entirely observable, or not relationally complex, structure may provide only marginal gain or even introduce unnecessary overhead.
If graph extraction is low quality or introduces consistent errors, structured memory may not outperform simpler baselines.

supporting_evidence.text: AriGraph's hybrid semantic + episodic graph memory enables Ariadne agents to outperform full-history, summarization, RAG, Simulacra, and Reflexion baselines on TextWorld and matches the performance of full-level-observation or NetPlay-memory-oracle agents in NetHack.

GATA's continuous belief graph outperforms text-only or RNN/hybrid memory baselines (Tr-DQN, Tr-DRQN, episodic count) and achieves large relative improvement (+20–24%) in generalization to unseen games.

KG-A2C (and derivatives) using knowledge graphs as structured memory deliver substantially faster convergence and higher scores compared to RL baselines lacking graph memory, especially on hard, partially observable IF games.
Attribute-based or graph-diff memory (MemInsight, Worldformer) produce large improvements over RAG-type baselines or unstructured episodic storage on QA and event summarization tasks.
Simulacra/generative agent style unstructured episodic memory achieves only partial gains—AriGraph outperforms it, especially for tasks requiring planning/navigation via explicit semantic structure.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Knowledge graphs and structured memory are used individually in agent architectures and RL literature, but are often discussed as implementation details, not as a general property required for long-horizon generalization.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: This theory asserts that structure in memory (graph, attribute, semantic linkages) is the critical common factor for robust generalization and multi-step reasoning, above and beyond the effect of agent architecture alone.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Though structured memory is implemented in individual works, the generality of this as a law for scalable text-game planning/generalization is not formalized in the LLM agent field.
references: Adhikari et al. (2020) Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs [Belief graphs for RL]
Papazian et al. (2024) AriGraph [Semantic-episodic graphs in LLM agents]
Meng et al. (2025) MemInsight [Attribute-based structure in memory augmentation]

theory_name: Structured Semantic Memory (Knowledge Graphs and Attribute-augmented Episodic Stores) is Essential for Long-horizon Planning and Generalization in LLM Text Game Agents
theory_description: LLM agents provided with structured, semantic memory formats—such as knowledge graphs (explicitly or via belief graphs), hybrid semantic-episodic graphs, or attribute-annotated episodic memory—enable richer, multi-hop reasoning about state, navigation, and plans than either unstructured episodic or pure summary memory. The graph or attribute structure not only supports efficient navigation and state tracking in partially observable games, but also enables multi-step reasoning, ensures consistency across steps, and enhances the generalization of agent policies to novel environments and tasks.

law.statement_name: Hybrid Semantic-Episodic or Attribute-augmented Memories Enable State Tracking and Multi-hop Retrieval under Partial Observability
law.theory_statement: Hybrid semantic-episodic memory, or storage of attributes and fact-linked episodes, enables LLM agents to maintain state and plan even with limited local observations or restricted context windows, outperforming both fully unstructured episodic memory and naive summarization in navigation, multi-step planning, and partially observed games.
law.domain_scope: Text environments with partial observability, dynamic state (where state is not re-observable after leaving), or high multi-hop inference demands—such as NetHack, TextWorld, Light, and open-ended game benchmarks.
special_cases: If the episodic memory alone suffices due to strict task locality or agent is repeatedly reset to an initial state, hybrid/attribute augmentation gains are marginal.
If attribute/semantic extraction is faulty or inconsistent, hybrid methods can underperform or be misleading.

supporting_evidence.text: Ariadne's episodic-augmented AriGraph achieves parity with memory-oracle NetPlay baselines despite only receiving localized observations; ablation removing episodic vertices in AriGraph leads to severe performance drops on instruction-heavy tasks (Cooking, Treasure Hunt Hard).

MemInsight's attribute-based retrieval and embedding yields much higher recall, F1, and QA accuracy than non-structured RAG or summary memory; attribute-level augmentation improves context efficiency and retrieval quality.

Simulacra/kindred unstructured episodic memory aids recency/importance, but fails at complex multi-hop tasks like Cleaning or planning navigation, where AriGraph's semantic links allow better performance.
Summary and RAG baselines in AriGraph paper perform better than full-history but are always inferior to AriGraph on partially observable or multi-step relational reasoning tasks.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Hybrid semantic-episodic memory is available in some recent cognitive architectures and graph/RAG systems, but its superiority in planning, navigation, and partial observability is typically shown in isolated results, not formulated as a central prerequisite for robust agent memory.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Generalizes episodic/semantic augmentation as a universal requirement for partial observability and non-myopic planning, connecting to working memory theories from cognitive science and symbolic AI.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Somewhat related to prior KGs and hybrid memory models, but newly formalized as a high-level law for the specific task of LLM agent effectiveness and generalization.
references: Papazian et al. (2024) AriGraph [Hybrid graph memory]
Meng et al. (2025) MemInsight [Attribute-based episodic-semantic retrieval]

theory_name: Structured, Task-Specific and Polymodal Memory Architectures Supercharge LLM Agents in Text Games
theory_description: This theory asserts that language model agents in text-based games achieve superior generalization, planning, and long-horizon performance when their memory systems are structured and tailored to the needs of the game: (1) explicit knowledge graphs (semantic and episodic, e.g., AriGraph, GATA) for tracking entities and relations under partial observability; (2) retrieval-augmented episodic memory (e.g., RAP, MC-DML, ThinkThrice, Generative Agents) for analogical in-context learning, especially in open domains and non-repeatable tasks; and (3) compressed or summarized representations (e.g., diff-history, area selection, iterative summarization, Simulacra) for scaling under token or compute constraints. Structuring memory to both the game environment and agent’s cognitive demands—such as planning, exploration, and multi-agent social reasoning—enables (a) resource-efficient action selection, (b) robust recovery from stochastic exploration, (c) generalization to unseen games or roles, and (d) multi-agent coherence.

law.theory_statement: LLM/text-game agents provided with semantically-structured external memory (e.g., dynamic knowledge graphs, summarized representations, area-scoped state) achieve higher generalization, sample efficiency, task success, and error reduction than agents with only unstructured, truncated, full-history, or short-window memory, especially in partially observable, multi-step, and/or large action/state space text games.
law.domain_scope: Applies to LLM-based or RL+LM agents in partially observable, multi-step, multi-object text-based games and environments where context-window or computational constraints limit effective memory; includes benchmarks like TextWorld, Jericho, NetHack, Goedendag, ScienceWorld, and open-ended role-playing.
special_cases: Games with trivial state/action spaces show less benefit.
When a perfect state oracle (e.g., ground-truth full graph) is available, further structuring may reach an empirical upper bound.

supporting_evidence.text: AriGraph's semantic+episodic memory substantially outperforms full-history, iterative summarization, RAG, Simulacra, Reflexion, and RL baselines across TextWorld tasks; knowledge-graph memory (KG-A2C, GATA) consistently beats text-only, n-gram, or recurrence-only baselines on Jericho and TextWorld.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Prior work advocates both structured (KGs) and unstructured (full-history, RAG, chain-of-thought) memory. Some combinations (e.g., AriGraph's episodic+semantic blend, MC-DML's planning memory) are new.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: This law unifies and generalizes from diverse, empirically head-to-head comparisons across agents and memory types, establishing a hierarchy and design rationale for structured, task-aligned memory.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: No prior work exhaustively formalizes this structuring-law across memory modalities and empirical agents in such breadth; existing works test isolated components.
references: Madotto et al. (2021) Learning Knowledge Graph-based World Models [KG-A2C, Worldformer]
Papazian et al. (2024) AriGraph [Hybrid memory, multi-setting comparison]

theory_name: Structured, Task-Specific and Polymodal Memory Architectures Supercharge LLM Agents in Text Games
theory_description: This theory asserts that language model agents in text-based games achieve superior generalization, planning, and long-horizon performance when their memory systems are structured and tailored to the needs of the game: (1) explicit knowledge graphs (semantic and episodic, e.g., AriGraph, GATA) for tracking entities and relations under partial observability; (2) retrieval-augmented episodic memory (e.g., RAP, MC-DML, ThinkThrice, Generative Agents) for analogical in-context learning, especially in open domains and non-repeatable tasks; and (3) compressed or summarized representations (e.g., diff-history, area selection, iterative summarization, Simulacra) for scaling under token or compute constraints. Structuring memory to both the game environment and agent’s cognitive demands—such as planning, exploration, and multi-agent social reasoning—enables (a) resource-efficient action selection, (b) robust recovery from stochastic exploration, (c) generalization to unseen games or roles, and (d) multi-agent coherence.

law.theory_statement: LLM-based text-game agents using retrieval-augmented episodic memory (e.g., successful trajectory windows, experience or reflection banks) achieve large, often superlinear, gains over vanilla ReAct, reflection-only, or prompt-context agents in environments with variable or one-shot tasks, creative/explorative requirements, or multi-agent hypothesis formation.
law.domain_scope: Applies to LLM-based and planning agents operating in multi-task/multi-domain text games (ALFWorld, WebShop, ScienceWorld, Jubensha, social simulation, e-commerce, open-ended role-play) where memory must generalize across instance/task boundaries and supports in-context analogical reasoning.
special_cases: Tasks with no transfer or analogy potential (fully deterministic, fixed tasks) may gain less.
Effectiveness depends on retrieval quality (e.g., embedding, weighting, plan/window selection).

supporting_evidence.text: RAP’s retrieval of contextually similar successful episodes enables GPT-3.5/GPT-4/Llama2-13b to outperform ReAct and Reflexion on ALFWorld/WebShop; MC-DML’s dual memory greatly boosts MCTS performance on Jericho games; ThinkThrice’s memory-retrieved, self-refined, and self-verified multi-agent LLMs in Jubensha far surpass no-memory baselines in factual recall and collective reasoning; Generative Agents' memory/plan/reflection pipeline yields highly believable, coherent social behavior.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Experience-replay and episodic reflection (e.g., Reflexion) are known. RAP’s cross-task memory, ThinkThrice's embedding-based, and MC-DML’s MCTS with memory-augmented priors are recent and not fully synthesized previously.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: This law generalizes comparative findings that selective, retrieval-augmented episodic memory (beyond reflection) enables analogical reasoning, curriculum transfer, and creative hypothesis formation that vanilla LLM prompting or first-order memory cannot.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Synthesis and explicit law are new, though individual components are present in recent works.
references: Shinn et al. (2023) Reflexion [Failure-centric episodic memory]
Papazian et al. (2024) RAP [Retrieval-Augmented Planning, cross-task memory]
Zhao et al. (2024) MC-DML [Dual-memory for MCTS]
Liu et al. (2023) ThinkThrice [Retrieval + self-refinement/verification]

theory_name: Compression and Selection Mechanisms Are Essential to Overcome Scaling Limits for In-Context Memory in LLM Text-Game Agents
theory_description: This theory posits that as text-game environments scale in observation length, object count, and task horizon, LLM and RL-based agents must rely on memory compression (e.g., diff-history, summarization, area selection) and relevance-based selection (e.g., retrieval, recency/importance scoring) rather than full retention of episode histories in prompt context. Without these, agents hit hard resource and sample efficiency ceilings due to context-window length, token costs, and cognitive overload. Compression must not discard critical state changes; selection must ensure salient, task-relevant cues are retained.

law.statement_name: Compression and Relevance Law for Scalable LLM Memory
law.theory_statement: When environment text or underlying state grows faster than available LLM/RL context capacity, agents employing memory compression (e.g., diff-history, summarization, area-pruning) and relevance-based selection (e.g., scoring by recency, importance, embedding similarity) maintain or improve performance compared to full-history or fixed-window approaches, and do so with lower compute, lower error rate, and greater sample efficiency.
law.domain_scope: Applies to LLM-based and RL+LM agents in high-dimensional, long-horizon, or multi-step domains where prompt token or recurrence-hidden-state memory is limiting, including NetHack (LangHack), TextWorld, Goedendag, and real-world multi-step/embodied text settings.
special_cases: Low-dimensional or toy games may not require compression.
Compression methods must preserve critical information; lossy/over-aggressive compression can degrade reasoning.

supporting_evidence.text: Diff-history agents greatly outperform full-history on NetHack (e18.0, e18.1, e18.2); area-selection and summarization improve Goedendag win-rate and error reduction (e12.0, e12.1, e12.2). Full-history and in-context-only agents perform poorly on long/hard TextWorld variants and rapidly hit prompt limits (e5.2). Score-based selection (Simulacra, MemInsight), attribute-based filtering and embedding retrieval (e23.0), and pruning of action/state space (KG-A2C masking, e11.3) consistently reduce errors and improve efficiency.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Summarization and retrieval have been used in static QA; incremental compression (diff-history, area selection) and their necessity across game scaling are more recent.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: This law quantifies and generalizes the performance/efficiency gains (≥ 4x–10x, e.g., NetHack, Goedendag) and their centrality for scaling to large/hard environments, showing catastrophic failure of non-compressed approaches.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: While summarization has precedent, the comparative empirical law and explicit performance/capacity bounds are new.
references: Papazian et al. (2023) diff History for Neural Language Agents [diff-history compression]
He et al. (2024) MemInsight [attribute-based/embedding retrieval]
Lin et al. (2023) Generative Agents [score-based selection]
Zhao et al. (2024) MC-DML [dual memory, capacity constraints]
Papazian et al. (2024) Goedendag [area selection, summarization]

theory_name: Hybrid Structured and Episodic Memory Enables Robust, Generalizable LLM Agent Performance in Text Games
theory_description: Language model agents for text-based games achieve superior robustness, sample efficiency, and generalization when augmented with hybrid memory systems that combine explicit structured (e.g., knowledge-graph or belief-graph) state representations and episodic (trajectory or reflection-based) memory. Structured memory provides compact, relational representations that support multi-hop reasoning, navigation, and action-space pruning, while episodic memory enables recall of detailed instructions, procedures, and successful/failed experiences. The synergistic integration and principled retrieval of both kinds of memories enables agents to overcome both partial observability and long-horizon credit assignment bottlenecks, outperforming agents with either memory alone, with unstructured memory (e.g., full text history), or with naive retrieval/summarization.

law.theory_statement: When both structured (e.g., knowledge-graph/semantic) and episodic (e.g., observation/trajectory/reflective) memory are available and purposefully integrated and retrieved into agent working memory, LLM-based agents achieve higher normalized/game performance, reduced error rates, and greater sample efficiency and generalization compared to agents using only one memory type or only unstructured full-history/retrieval.
law.domain_scope: Applies to LLM agents in text-based games with partial observability, combinatorial action spaces, and complex state transitions, especially in environments requiring multi-step planning, navigation, and long-term instruction following (e.g., TextWorld, NetHack, general text-based tasks)
special_cases: Games solvable via short-term context or trivial observation-to-action mapping may show little benefit.
If memory construction (e.g., triplet extraction) is highly unreliable, benefits are reduced/disrupted.

supporting_evidence.text: Ariadne/AriGraph (hybrid graph: semantic+episodic) achieves perfect or near-perfect normalized score on most TextWorld tasks, outperforming single-memory ablations (no episodic, Simulacra-style only, RAG, full-history, summarization) especially in tasks needing procedural recall and planning.

Structured graph-based memory (GATA, Worldformer, KG-A2C) consistently outperforms text-only, full-history, or RNN-memory baselines across multiple text games, and agents with both semantic and episodic memory (AriGraph) handle richer objectives.

Episodic or reflection memory (MC-DML, RAP, Reflexion, Sweet&Sour, Simulacra, ThinkThrice) provides significant gains over non-reflective or non-retrieval agents, but combining with structured semantic retrieval (AriGraph) yields further gains in hard tasks.
RAG-style unstructured retrieval or full-history context produces lower normalized scores and higher error rates versus structured knowledge-graph/episodic hybrids in TextWorld, NetHack, and others.
ML agents using only summarization or full uncompressed history are outperformed by those using structured memory or hybrid structured-episodic systems.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Structured memory (KG/belief-graphs), episodic/retrieval/self-reflective memory, and RAG have each been used individually in various agents.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Formalized principle and empirical demonstration that the controlled hybridization (joint presence and integration) of both structured and episodic memory is necessary and sufficient for top performance/generalization in complex text games.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: While past work uses each memory type separately or in informal hybrid forms, explicit hybridization with task-driven retrieval is new as a unifying theory across these benchmarks.
references: Papazian et al. (2024) AriGraph [hybrid KG+episodic memory architecture and empirical benefit]
Yao et al. (2020) GATA [KG/belief-graph structured memory]
Zhou et al. (2023) Generative Agents [episodic memory + importance/relevance/recency, but not structured semantic hybridization]
Shinn et al. (2023;2024) Reflexion [episodic reflection memory but not semantic hybrid]
Hao et al. (2024) Sweet&Sour [reflection memory with positive/negative experience]

theory_name: Hybrid Structured and Episodic Memory Enables Robust, Generalizable LLM Agent Performance in Text Games
theory_description: Language model agents for text-based games achieve superior robustness, sample efficiency, and generalization when augmented with hybrid memory systems that combine explicit structured (e.g., knowledge-graph or belief-graph) state representations and episodic (trajectory or reflection-based) memory. Structured memory provides compact, relational representations that support multi-hop reasoning, navigation, and action-space pruning, while episodic memory enables recall of detailed instructions, procedures, and successful/failed experiences. The synergistic integration and principled retrieval of both kinds of memories enables agents to overcome both partial observability and long-horizon credit assignment bottlenecks, outperforming agents with either memory alone, with unstructured memory (e.g., full text history), or with naive retrieval/summarization.

law.theory_statement: The proportional benefit of structured versus episodic memory modalities is task-dependent: procedural and instruction-heavy tasks see greatest improvement from episodic memory, navigation and spatial reasoning benefit most from structured semantic (graph) memory, and multi-agent social/coordination tasks require both—hence, dynamic or adaptive use of memory types yields maximal robustness.
law.domain_scope: LLM/ML agents in partially observable text games/environments with heterogenous task structure (e.g., TextWorld variants, NetHack, Smallville simulations)
special_cases: Tasks that are trivially solved via immediate observable context may show minimal sensitivity to memory modality.
If the environment is fully observed (oracle), structured memory adds little.

supporting_evidence.text: Ariadne ablation (no episodic) is near-perfect on Cleaning (navigation/state-tracking) but degrades on Cooking and Treasure Hunt Hard (long procedural instructions), showing relative importance of memory type per task.
Simulacra (episodic-only) memory is outperformed by AriGraph on navigation/planning tasks; Simulacra matches or exceeds AriGraph on tasks emphasizing immediate/easy recent recall (e.g., Cleaning).
Full-level oracle observation (NetPlay Level obs) is matched by AriGraph using only Room obs + hybrid memory; Room-only ablation without external memory loses substantial performance.
Summary/area-based memory improves error rate and win rate primarily in environments with high branching/planning complexity.

Hierarchical memory/retrieval and importance/recency scoring in multi-agent contexts (Generative Agents, ThinkThrice) yield improved believability and inference compared to non-retrieval or naively structured agents.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Task-dependent retrieval/attention and use of specialized memory is conceptually known in cognitive/AI literature, but rarely formalized with explicit empirical partitioning in text-game LLMs.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Demonstration and articulation of environment/task type dictating required memory modality and empirical boundaries in LLM-based agents is novel.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Some agents select or weight memory components per task, but evidence-based division of roles by environment/task type is not formalized in prior text-game LLM works.
references: Papazian et al. (2024) AriGraph [empirical ablations by task]
Park et al. (2023) Generative Agents [multi-agent/simulation benefit from episodic/hierarchical memory]
ThinkThrice (2023) [retrieval + verification in social deduction]

theory_name: Reflective and Retrieval-Augmented Planning Substantially Improves LLM Agent Adaptability and Sample Efficiency
theory_description: LLM-based game agents that are augmented with memory mechanisms supporting reflection (self-critique and synthesis of experience) and retrieval of relevant past trajectories (episodic or plan-based) show large and transferable improvements in solving diverse, complex, multi-step text games, especially ones with sparse rewards or creative bottlenecks. Integrating reflection/retrieval-based memory with action planning enables agents to avoid repeated failures, inject analogical knowledge from previous successful attempts, and bootstrap future behavior, in ways that pure model-based or direct RL-based agents cannot.

law.theory_statement: Agents that retrieve and incorporate relevant past trajectories (from external episodic memory or demonstration banks) and that perform self-reflection (verbalizing reasons for failures and successes) as persistent memory achieve greater adaptability (across tasks, reward functions, and unseen domains), sample efficiency, and success rates than agents relying solely on in-context chain-of-thought, direct RL, or unaugmented LLM planning.
law.domain_scope: Cooperative or competitive text-based game environments with long or partially observable trajectories, combinatorial actions, and limited supervised demonstrations (e.g., ALFWorld, WebShop, ScienceWorld, Jericho, Smallville/Simulacra)
special_cases: If past trajectories are functionally irrelevant (fully out-of-distribution), retrieval may not help.
Reflection memory may have negative transfer if reflections are poorly evaluated or the environment shifts substantially.

supporting_evidence.text: RAP (retrieval of successful episodic trajectories) boosts ALFWorld/WebShop agent success rates by ~30-35% over ReAct baseline and outperforms both Reflexion (reflection-only) and ADaPT (decomposition/re-execution) benchmarks; RAP memory generalizes to new model architectures (e.g., Llama2-13b) and supports cross-modal retrieval plans.

MC-DML (in-trial + cross-trial episodic reflections as memory) sets new upper bounds on early planning performance in Jericho/IF games, with performance gains of 10-30 points over pure LLM-MCTS, Reflection-only, and LLM baselines; ablation shows both reflection and retrieval are needed for full effect.

Sweet&Sour (reflection on both successes and failures with managed memory buffer) yields higher success rates in ScienceWorld across all tested LLMs than failure-only reflection (Reflexion) or no-reflection baselines, with larger gains for smaller models.

Generative Agents (Simulacra) and ThinkThrice (retrieval + self-verification) show that externally stored experience, retrieval and structured reflection, and plan synthesis all contribute to emergent, coherent, and generalizable behavior in open yet partially observable environments.

In many settings (ALFWorld, WebShop), Reflexion/failure-only reflection improves over pure LLM baselines but is outperformed by retrieval-augmented or full episodic memory architectures (RAP, MC-DML, Sweet&Sour).

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Reflection (Reflexion), retrieval (RAG, RAP), and plan-based retrieval exist separately in prior LLM agent literature.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: The law asserts that memory banks supporting both reflection (verbal self-critique) and retrieval of relevant past trajectories/plans are not just helpful but strictly necessary for rapid adaptation and transfer in multi-step text games—supported across multiple environments and agent designs.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Though reflection and retrieval exist individually, the theoretical unification, task demonstrations, and role in transfer/generalization are new.
references: Shinn et al. (2023) Reflexion [reflection/intra-task memory]
Papazian et al. (2024) RAP [retrieval of past episodic trajectories improves performance and generalization]
Hao et al. (2024) Sweet&Sour [reflection on both positive and negative experience with buffer management]
Zhou et al. (2023) Generative Agents [memory + reflection + plan synthesis in multi-agent simulations]

theory_name: Reflective and Retrieval-Augmented Planning Substantially Improves LLM Agent Adaptability and Sample Efficiency
theory_description: LLM-based game agents that are augmented with memory mechanisms supporting reflection (self-critique and synthesis of experience) and retrieval of relevant past trajectories (episodic or plan-based) show large and transferable improvements in solving diverse, complex, multi-step text games, especially ones with sparse rewards or creative bottlenecks. Integrating reflection/retrieval-based memory with action planning enables agents to avoid repeated failures, inject analogical knowledge from previous successful attempts, and bootstrap future behavior, in ways that pure model-based or direct RL-based agents cannot.

law.statement_name: Positive and Negative Experience Reflection Principle
law.theory_statement: Providing agents with managed memory buffers containing both positive (successful subgoal) and negative (failure) reflections enhances learning, resilience, and out-of-distribution adaptability more substantially than failure-only reflective memory, especially in long-horizon or sparse-reward settings.
law.domain_scope: LLM-based agents in environments with extended episode lengths, subgoal structure, or delayed/non-stationary rewards (ScienceWorld, Jericho tasks, open-ended text-adventure/bot games).
special_cases: Reflection content must be accurate—if the agent's evaluation is unreliable or subgoal delineation is unavailable, the benefits of positive memory may not materialize.
If the environment is fully observable and trivial, positive/negative reflection may offer little incremental benefit.
For tasks demanding novelty/discovery (as opposed to repetition or analogy), prior reflection could sometimes hinder exploration (negative transfer).

supporting_evidence.text: Sweet&Sour's dual-buffer reflection (positive+negative) outperforms fail-only ablation and Reflexion on ScienceWorld—GPT-4o, Mistral Large 2, and Llama 8B all achieve higher average scores when both positive and negative experiences are reflected on and retrieved.
Reflexion tilts or gets stuck once initial success is achieved (cannot reinforce positives, neglects adaptation), Sweet&Sour avoids this pitfall.
MC-DML's cross-trial reflection, which incorporates both types of lesson, yields stronger ablation gains than reflection-only (failure-only) or prompt-only baselines in Jericho text games.
Simulacra baseline (inspired by Generative Agents' reasoning on importance/recency/success) outperforms simple context/retrieval on emergent behaviors.
Agents lacking memory of positive outcomes (fail-only) regress to less robust/tilted search, per-case and cross-paper demonstration.

novelty_evaluation.what_already_exists: Reflection on failure (Reflexion) exists, and separate positive experience utilization is common in traditional RL (reward shaping, memory buffers), but explicit positive/negative verbal reflection managed memory is novel for LLMs.
novelty_evaluation.what_is_novel: Demonstrating and formalizing the need for both positive and negative experience reflection (and their retrieval) as external, persistent memory in LLM agent architectures, including their impact on generalization and adaptation.
novelty_evaluation.classification_explanation: Combination of both types of reflection in managed external memory for LLMs is novel and shown to be performance-critical.
references: Hao et al. (2024) Sweet&Sour [explicit dual-buffer reflection, demonstration of necessity]
Shinn et al. (2023,2024) Reflexion [failure-only reflection as precedent, shown to be less effective]
